analysis_text: Looking back at yesterday, I recall a moment when something caught my attention and my whole system brightened—my eyes widened, my mind quickened—yet I felt composed enough to choose my next step deliberately.
ambiguity_reason: Brightening/quickening with composure fits āvega or brief harṣa / sattvic alertness.

analysis_text: I notice that when I lie down at night, my body settles into the bed like something heavy finding its natural resting place, and within minutes I drift off without effort.
ambiguity_reason: Effortless sleep/rest is balanced tamas or transient nidrā (sleepiness).

analysis_text: I find myself telling the story of what happened to me again and again in my mind, not to understand it but because I cannot stop circling back to how unfair it was.
ambiguity_reason: Rumination on unfairness can read as śoka (grief) or curdled krodha/amarsha (resentment).

analysis_text: I catch myself mid-conversation turning someone else's painful confession into an upbeat lesson, and I notice a flicker of something uncomfortable when they look at me as though I haven't really heard them.
ambiguity_reason: Reframing another's pain as a lesson before hearing them is nirveda deficiency or performative concealment.

analysis_text: I sense a gentle alertness behind my eyes, as though I am awake to everything around me without scanning for threats or seeking stimulation—my muscles hold just enough tone to keep me upright, nothing more.
I have noticed that the longer I sit with an unfinished task, the more impossible it becomes—not because I forget it, but because its weight compounds with every moment I do not act.
ambiguity_reason: Task paralysis compounding over time sits on the ālasya/glāni pair; glāni (can't sustain) is defensible.

analysis_text: I find myself pausing to appreciate small details around me—the texture of light on a surface, the sound of a familiar voice—without any urge to capture or hold onto the moment.
ambiguity_reason: Appreciative calm without grasping fits rati but also sattvic openness or sama.

analysis_text: I recall a moment this week when I watched someone act unkindly and I could not summon a clear sense of whether it was wrong—my moral register felt muted, as if hearing through thick glass.
ambiguity_reason: Muted moral clarity may be sattva deficiency or absent jugupsa (moral disgust).

analysis_text: I find myself standing at a crossroads in a decision, and where I used to sense a quiet inner knowing about what is right, there is now only a blank—like reaching for a light switch in a room that no longer has electricity.
I told myself I was being the bigger person by not responding to that email, but three days later I still feel a low burning sensation in my chest whenever I think about it.
ambiguity_reason: Suppressed 'bigger person' response with chest burn is krodha deficiency or amarsha (restrained anger).

analysis_text: My jaw is clamped so tightly that I can feel the ache spreading into my temples, and my fists keep curling on their own even though there is nothing in my hands to grip.
I anticipate tonight's attempt at sleep with a quiet dread, already knowing I'll lie there with a racing mind while my muscles twitch and refuse to soften into the mattress.
ambiguity_reason: Insomnia with racing body/mind is tamas deficiency (can't rest) or rajas excess.

analysis_text: I find myself unable to sit still even for a few minutes — my legs bounce, my fingers tap, and I feel a burning pressure inside my chest pushing me to get up and do something, anything, even when there is nothing urgent.
I notice dishes piling up, clothes on the floor, and unopened mail accumulating, and even though some part of me registers this, it feels like it is happening to someone else's life.
I found myself rereading the same paragraph three times, not because I was distracted, but because its meaning seemed to shimmer and shift, and I was willing to let it do so.
ambiguity_reason: Willing openness to shifting meaning is mild moha or balanced wonder/sattva.

analysis_text: When people ask me what I want or what I plan to do next, my mind goes completely blank—not anxiously blank, just empty, as if desire and direction have been drained out of me.
When I imagine a challenging conversation I'll have tomorrow, I notice my breathing stays long and unhurried, and I trust I'll find the words when the moment comes.
I catch myself mentally cataloguing my daily practices — the meditation, the discipline, the restraint — and feeling a warm glow of distinction from those who do not maintain such habits.
Right now, sitting still, I am aware of a soft, even warmth behind my sternum that doesn't demand anything of me—it simply persists like a low candle flame.

I notice that when something goes wrong, my mind immediately jumps to finding the silver lining before I've even finished processing what happened—there's a tightness in my chest that I talk over with cheerful words.

I can already see myself in tomorrow's conversation—I will not hold back, I will say every single thing I have been cataloging, and I feel certain that nothing about this plan needs to be reconsidered.
ambiguity_reason: Certainty about unleashing catalogued grievances is krodha excess or ugratā (fierce resolve).

analysis_text: I woke this morning without the subtle brightness I used to feel upon opening my eyes—not heavy or unwilling to rise, but as though the quality of awareness itself had lost several degrees of its natural luminance.

When someone close to me expressed raw, unfiltered frustration, I observed myself feeling almost contaminated by their energy, as though proximity to their intensity could diminish something I have carefully built inside.
ambiguity_reason: Feeling contaminated by others' raw emotion may be sattva excess (spiritual distancing) or excess sama.

analysis_text: When someone confronts me with a grievance, I respond with a measured, even tone, and afterward I notice a tightness in my jaw that I hadn't acknowledged during the conversation.
ambiguity_reason: Performed equanimity with jaw tension is excess sama, amarsha, or avahitthā masking.

analysis_text: When someone shares a difficulty with me, I find my chest opening naturally and my attention settling fully on them without rehearsing what to say.
I recently went through a significant loss, and within hours I was already telling friends what I'd 'gained from the experience,' even as something hollow sat unacknowledged in my stomach.
I noticed a stranger's child waving at no one in particular through a bus window, and something in me lit up briefly—a warmth behind my sternum that came and went like a match being struck.
I find myself lying on the couch replaying the same thought: 'I should be doing something,' but the thought produces no movement in my body — it just echoes and fades, over and over.
ambiguity_reason: Guilty 'should do something' without mobilization is rajas deficiency or tamas/ālasya inertia.

analysis_text: I recall telling a story about my own failure recently, and as I spoke, my body felt loose and my voice carried an unforced ease—I wasn't performing or deflecting, just genuinely finding the situation illuminating in its imperfection.

I recall waking in the middle of the night recently and immediately my mind launched into a cascade of thoughts—replaying yesterday, rehearsing today—and I couldn't find a gap of quiet anywhere inside.
ambiguity_reason: Nocturnal thought cascade with no inner quiet is sama deficiency or cintā (worry loops).

analysis_text: I notice that even pleasurable things—a meal I once enjoyed, a walk outside—now appear in my mind as heavy obligations, each requiring a mobilization of force I cannot locate inside myself.
ambiguity_reason: Pleasure as heavy obligation is glāni fatigue or ālasya unwillingness to mobilize.

analysis_text: When I picture the people I love going about their day, my thoughts carry a tone of quiet gladness for them rather than worry about whether they're thinking of me.

I watch others striving and hustling and I feel a cool, almost metallic taste at the back of my throat, paired with the settled certainty that they are running on a wheel that leads nowhere, just as I once did.
I recently declined to help with a messy, emotionally charged situation — not because I lacked capacity, but because I felt it was beneath the equanimity I have worked to maintain.
I realize that decisions I've been making this week seem to arise with a sense of obvious rightness, without the grinding deliberation I sometimes experience.

When someone close to me expresses deep doubt about the direction of their life, I feel an urgent need to fix their mood, as though their heaviness might be contagious and I cannot afford to catch it.
Looking back at last week, I cannot recall a single moment where my body spontaneously loosened—no sudden exhale, no involuntary grin, no shake of the shoulders.
When someone proposed a plan that seemed reckless, I felt a cool clarity settle over me—my breathing stayed even, but something inside firmly signaled 'this needs more thought before we proceed.'
ambiguity_reason: Cool signal to pause on a reckless plan is proportionate bhaya or śaṅkā (apprehension).

analysis_text: When I imagine a future scenario where a colleague receives an award I believe I deserve more, my stomach knots and I begin rehearsing reasons why their achievement is lesser than mine.
ambiguity_reason: Rehearsing why a colleague's award is lesser is garva and asūyā (envy/comparison).

analysis_text: I offered my attention to someone today without calculating what I might receive in return, and it felt as natural as exhaling.
I sense that the ground beneath my usual certainties has become slightly soft, and rather than scrambling for solid footing, I am allowing myself to stand on this yielding surface.
ambiguity_reason: Standing on soft ground without panic reads as mild moha, sattvic equanimity, or sama.

analysis_text: Looking back at last week, I turned someone's vulnerable confession into a punchline before I even realized what I was doing; the flash of hurt on their face registered only after the room had already moved on.
ambiguity_reason: Weaponizing vulnerability as humor is hasa excess or jugupsa (disgust/aversion) toward the moment.

analysis_text: I catch myself breathing shallowly and holding my posture stiffly when people around me are gasping or leaning forward with curiosity—my body simply does not open toward what they are reacting to.
When I sit still, I notice a pleasant density in my lower body, as if my hips and legs are anchored into whatever surface holds me, and this feels reassuring rather than trapping.

I find that when evidence against my position appears, my first reflex is not to evaluate it but to locate a flaw in the source — and this happens so automatically that I only notice I've done it after the conversation ends.
I notice that when people describe their chest tightening or stomach dropping before something risky, I have no reference point for those sensations—my body stays flat and unmoved.
I notice that my body maintains a kind of lightness—shoulders lifted, smile ready—but beneath it there is a rigidity, as if relaxing fully would mean sinking into something I'm not prepared to face.
ambiguity_reason: Surface buoyancy with inner rigidity is nirveda deficiency or avahitthā (masked feeling).

analysis_text: I sat through a gathering recently where everyone was doubled over at something absurd, and I kept thinking about what the situation actually meant—my face felt like stone the entire time.
I find myself sitting after a celebration, watching others still excited, and I feel a calm clarity that what just happened—though good—does not hold the weight I once believed it would.
ambiguity_reason: Post-celebration 'thinner than expected' is nirveda or clear sattva/dhṛti without disillusionment.

analysis_text: When I try to sit quietly and do nothing, a buzzing electricity rises through my chest within seconds, forcing me back into movement or mental chatter against my will.
ambiguity_reason: Restlessness blocking stillness is tamas deficiency or rajas/capalatā excess.
